Author: E. Alsharaf; B. Alothman
Title: Towards Blockchain-Secured Agile Validation of Cyber Resilience in Industrial Control System Defence Environments
Publication Title: 2025 7th International Conference on Blockchain Computing and Applications (BCCA)
Abstract Note: Industrial Control Systems (ICS) play a crucial role in ensuring the safe and reliable operation of high-risk sectors, including the oil and gas industry. Their system is highly vulnerable to sophisticated cyberphysical threats, which can disrupt processes, compromise safety, and incur significant economic losses. Conventional security measures focus primarily on detecting and preventing these threats. Still, they offer minimal assurance of resilience (which refers to the system’s ability to anticipate, absorb, adapt, and recover from incidents). This research suggests the use of Blockchain-Enabled Agile Cyber Resilience (B-ACR) framework to integrate blockchain as a tamper-evident trust backbone within agile validation cycles. The proposed framework integrates dynamic IT/OT telemetry, Zero Trust enforcement, and Red/Blue Team simulation for the generation of measurable and auditable resilience indicators across the main pillars (PTO) (People, Technology, Organisation). This research utilises an oil and gas pipeline case study to demonstrate how blockchain anchoring can enhance forensic integrity while reducing mean time to detect and mean time to recover. It also highlights how this framework enables transparent maturity benchmarking. The results of this study suggest that blockchainsecured agile validation provides a verifiable and scalable trail for regulators, strengthening resilience in ICS defence environments.

Author: E. Haziz; R. Mohamed; M. F. M. Ghazali; M. H. F. Rahiman
Title: Instrument Reliability using Predictive Analytics (IRIS)
Publication Title: 2024 20th IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing & Its Applications (CSPA)
Abstract Note: The purpose of this research is to introduce IRIS, an Integrated Asset Analytics solution designed to offer real-time comprehensive insights and efficient workflow management for over 10,000 monitored devices, including sensors, valves, systems, and tanks since 2021. The motivation behind IRIS stems from the pressing need to address issues such as scattered data across multiple databases, leading to siloed information and a lack of visibility on critical equipment. By leveraging predictive analytics, IRIS aims to enhance maintenance planning, reduce operational and capital expenses, ensure compliance with safety standards for critical equipment, and minimize process safety incidents. The integrated approach facilitates data-driven decision-making, ultimately improving plant reliability and reducing unplanned downtime. This paper discusses the development and implementation of IRIS as an in-house cloud-based solution, highlighting its potential impact on instrument reliability and overall asset management. It provides valuable insights into the benefits and values of analytics, along with a brief module description and challenges during implementation. Through the use of predictive analytics, failure rate analysis, and early anomaly detection, IRIS aims to shift from a time-based to a predictive mindset culture, optimizing maintenance costs and avoiding deferment or shutdown. The implementation of IRIS offers significant benefits to complex operations, fleets, or networks of assets in the industry, including reductions in unplanned downtime, maintenance costs, and safety incidents, while also increasing asset availability and production productivity. In summary, IRIS presents a comprehensive solution to address challenges such as scattered data, lack of insights, and the growing need for integrated asset analytics in the industry.

Title: Digital Interconnected Intelligent Decision-Making Platform Based on AI Technology
Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Industrial Technology Applications (AIITA)
Abstract Note: In response to the ongoing digital transformation in the oil and gas industry, CNPCNP has developed the “Digital Interconnected Intelligent Decision-Making Platform” to enhance production efficiency, optimize operations, reduce costs, strengthen safety controls, and drive the high-quality development of the company. This platform integrates various AI technologies, including mining technology, business intelligence (BI), robotic process automation (RPA), optical character recognition (OCR), machine learning, low-code development, and AI-generated content (AIGC). By leveraging industrial interconnection, the platform creates a unified database that spans the entire data lifecycle, providing a comprehensive governance and data intelligence system. It effectively connects isolated data silos and enables the creation of advanced intelligent data models with minimal coding through “drag-and-drop” methods. This new approach to digitalization promotes a value framework of “connected by digitalization, driven by digitalization, and reshaped through digitalization.” Ultimately, the platform achieves key objectives, including paperless operations across all company processes, online processing of all workflows, automated information transmission, real-time monitoring of all data, interconnected project management and early warning systems, and overall intelligent company management.

Author: J. Krause; A. C. A. Beiruth; J. P. Barddal; A. S. Britto; V. M. A. Souza
Title: Fuels Demand Forecasting: Identifying Leading Feature Sets, Prediction Strategy, and Regressors
Publication Title: 2024 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
Abstract Note: Fuels are crucial for any country's development and economy, impacting various sectors such as transportation, industry, and electricity generation. Accurate prediction of monthly fuel demand can improve supply chain management, strategic decision-making, and financial planning for businesses while helping governments develop decarbonization policies and estimate pollutant emissions. This paper explores machine learning models to forecast fossil fuels and biofuel demand 12 months ahead, using univariate time series data representing the historical sales of 27 Brazilian states, one of the world's leading producers and consumers of fuels. We evaluate different time series feature sets, machine learning regression models, and prediction strategies to address the complexity of fuel sales influenced by factors such as economic conditions and geopolitical events. Our comprehensive evaluation aims to determine an effective setting for predictive models in the fuel domain. Our results show that popular feature extractors for time series, such as Catch22 and TsFresh, cannot improve the original data representation for most forecasting models. Although focused on Brazil, our findings apply to other countries, since the trained models do not rely on external variables, such as micro and macroeconomic indicators.

Author: Y. J. Ann; Z. Ishak; A. S. Zolkafly; F. S. M. Samsamnun; G. C. Meng; N. S. Jamil
Title: A Predictive Readiness Model for Blockchain Adoption in the Malaysian Palm Oil Industry
Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Sciences (AiDAS)
Abstract Note: This study explores the readiness of organizations within Malaysia's palm oil industry to adopt blockchain technology (BCT) for circular economy (CE) practices. By integrating the Technology-Organisation-Environment (TOE) and Human-Organisation-Technology (HOT) fit frameworks, we developed a comprehensive theoretical model supported by quantitative methods. A dataset of 186 survey responses was analyzed using linear regression, decision tree, and random forest models to identify the relative influence of technological, organizational, environmental, and human factors. The findings show that constructs from the Technology Acceptance Model, especially perceived ease of use, perceived usefulness, and perceived behavioral control, play significant roles in determining adoption readiness. An interactive tool developed using React further enables stakeholders to simulate and visualize adoption scenarios. The study provides a substantial predictive readiness framework by combining machine learning analysis and TOE and HOT frameworks. A decision-support tool was also created to guide the stakeholders through a visualization of adoption situations.

Author: R. Ghanbarifard; A. H. Almeida; A. Azevedo
Title: Digital Twin in complex operations environments: potential applications and research challenges
Abstract Note: This paper aims to thoroughly discuss the use of Digital Twin technology in complex operations environments, highlighting its potential applications and the research challenges that need to be addressed. This is necessitated by the fact that currently there is no comprehensive literature review and framework for implementing Digital Twin technology in complex operations environments. Furthermore, existing interpretations of DT implementation are inadequately detailed and not very informative in this area. This may be a consequence of the difficulties of collecting and extracting useful information from data in real-time. Another drawback worth mentioning is that Digital twins at the moment center on an individual or isolated part instead of integrating the whole system and no current work talks about this holistic approach. This paper will focus on Digital Twins in complex operations environments and their applications. A review of scientific literature on the use of Digital Twins in complex operations environments is performed and the articles are categorized by the problems and challenges that they address requiring DT as a solution. A selection of papers that focus on this topic and represent the current situation of research will be emphasized. In conclusion, this work will be utilized as a baseline study to propose a Digital Twin reference framework, which eventually leads to implementing and evaluating a comprehensive Digital Twin methodology in complex systems.

Author: R. Dzhusupova; J. Bosch; H. H. Olsson
Title: Challenges in developing and deploying AI in the engineering, procurement and construction industry
Publication Title: 2022 IEEE 46th Annual Computers, Software, and Applications Conference (COMPSAC)
Abstract Note: AI in the Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) industry has not yet a proven track record in large-scale projects. Since AI solutions for industrial applications became available only recently, deployment experience and lessons learned are still to be built up. Several research papers exist describing the potential of AI, and many surveys and white papers have been published indicating the challenges of AI deployment in the EPC industry. However, there is a recognizable shortage of in-depth studies of deployment experience in academic literature, particularly those focusing on the experiences of EPC companies involved in large-scale project execution with high safety standards, such as the petrochemical or energy sector. The novelty of this research is that we explore in detail the challenges and obstacles faced in developing and deploying AI in a large-scale project in the EPC industry based on real-life use cases performed in an EPC company. Those identified challenges are not linked to specific technology or a company's know-how and, therefore, are universal. The findings in this paper aim to provide feedback to academia to reduce the gap between research and practice experience. They also help reveal the hidden stones when implementing AI solutions in the industry.

Author: A. Khan; M. Dutta
Title: Securing Industrial Networks: An Ensemble Learning Approach to Trojan Prevention in Digital Information Transfer
Publication Title: 2024 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Emerging Communication Technologies (ICEC)
Abstract Note: The industry relies heavily on the highly efficient operational processes required to guarantee the safe extraction, refinement, and distribution of such vital natural resources. The significance of ensuring the security of smart devices in the industries is evident, as there have been numerous cyber threats lately, such as Trojan attacks, that put industrial data at risk. The preservation of data is crucial for making informed decisions and improving the operational efficiency within this business. Machine learning offers a proactive and flexible approach to tackling the complex detection requirements due to the variability in the Trojan attack data and the increasing number of threats. This paper explores an experimental study of the possibilities of the Ensemble Learning approach to enhance security in the oil and gas sector for the specific case of Android-based devices. The research focuses on reaching high ROC-AUC scores known to be useful in evaluating how effectively a model can distinguish between legitimate and harmful behavior on the network. The studied work has great potential for influencing the improvement of the security of the industries by using machine learning-trained servers for communication to differentiate authorized from non-authorized users. Ensemble Learning is crucial for ensuring that the industry’s smart devices are protected from Trojan attacks and the data is secured. The relevance of this article is based on the significance of the oil and gas industry for the economy and its growing dependence on using advanced technologies and going digital.

Author: K. Hu; R. Levi; R. Yahalom; E. G. Zerhouni
Title: Supply Chain Characteristics as Predictors of Cyber Risk: A Machine-Learning Assessment
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
Abstract Note: This article provides the first large-scale data-driven analysis to evaluate the predictive power of digital supply chain attributes for assessing risk of cyberattack data breaches. Motivated by rapid increase in the complexity of digital supply chains and related third-party enabled cyberattacks, the article provides the first quantitative empirical evidence that digital supply-chain attributes are significant predictors of enterprise cyber risk. The analysis leverages externally observable cybersecurity ratings that aim to capture the quality of the enterprise internal cybersecurity management, but augments these with original supply chain features that are inspired by observed third-party cyberattack scenarios, as well as concepts from network science research. The main quantitative result of the article is to show that these supply chain network features add significant detection power to predicting enterprise cyber risk, relative to merely using enterprise-only attributes. In particular, compared to a base model that relies only on internal enterprise features, the supply chain network features improve the out-of-sample AUC by 2.3%. Given that a cyber data breach on a specific enterprise is a low probability high impact risk event, these improvements in the prediction power have significant value. Additionally, the model highlights several cybersecurity risk drivers related to third-party cyberattack and breach mechanisms and provides important insights as to what key metrics should be monitored and what interventions might be effective to mitigate these risks.

Author: M. Agrawal; O. Nagpurey; K. Dhote
Title: Harnessing Big Data for Price Predictions in Oil and Gas Markets
Publication Title: 2024 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Microelectronics, Automation, Computing and Communications Systems (ICMACC)
Abstract Note: This study aims to construct a robust forecasting model for predicting future prices of oil and gas resources, crucialfor diverse industries. Beginning with an exploration of foundational theories pertinent to oil and gas price forecasting, the research focuses on two pivotal forecast theories: the Target Capacity Utilization Principle (TCU) and the Theory of Exhaustible Resources. Employing a recursive simulation model grounded in the Target Capacity Utilization Rule and leveraging historical data spanning from 1987 to 2017, the study forecasts oil and gas prices from 1991 to 2017. The devised methodology results in a Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) of 12.676, Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 280.92, Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 0.2597, and Mean Percentage Error (MPE) of 0.028. Furthermore, the analysis is extended to monthly forecasts, yielding a MAD of 5.66, MSE of 82.1163, MAPE of 0.1246, and MPE of 0.038. These outcomes underscore the model's effectiveness, especially at the monthly forecasting level.

Author: Z. Ishak; A. S. A. Amran; M. D. Esfahani; R. K. Ayyasamy; M. O. B. Abdullah; Y. J. Ann
Title: Determining the Readiness Factors to Adapt Blockchain Technology in the Palm Oil Industry to Facilitate Circular Economy Transition
Publication Title: 2024 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Sciences (AiDAS)
Abstract Note: The palm oil industry plays a pivotal role in Malaysia's economy, yet it is facing significant challenges in meeting the mounting Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) standards. To address these challenges, promoting sustainable practices and creating a transparent and accountable supply chain is critical. Embracing circular economy (CE) principles offers a prime opportunity to reduce waste and recycle resources. In this regard, blockchain technology (BCT) can be instrumental in achieving the objective of integrating CE principles into the palm oil industry. Despite the existing research on BCT, the factors that facilitate its integration into the CE remain unclear. Therefore, a comprehensive understanding of cause-and-effect relationships and a decision-making framework are crucial for practitioners seeking to integrate BCT into their CE strategies. The lack of understanding poses a challenge for CE managers who want to adopt BCT, thus necessitating BCT readiness assessments in the palm oil industry. This research aims to develop a decision-making schema to enable practitioners to embrace BC technology into their supply chain strategies to further facilitate CE transition. The findings can help the palm oil industry integrate BC technology into their CE strategies, promoting sustainable production and consumption practices and reducing waste. Both readiness factors and measures are the significant outcomes of the research that achieves its objectives and contributions to further facilitate BC technology-powered CE of palm oil industry.

Author: N. G. Asnina; A. I. Kolosov; Y. V. Khitskova; K. A. Makoviy
Title: Intelligent Forecasting Methods in Choosing a Strategy for Participation in Public Procurement
Publication Title: 2023 5th International Conference on Control Systems, Mathematical Modeling, Automation and Energy Efficiency (SUMMA)
Abstract Note: The problem of choosing a strategy for a participating in public procurement in the form of electronic trading is considered. The formulation of possible strategies for potential participants is proposed, depending on the ratio of the predicted contract price and the cost of goods, works and services. The approach to choosing one of the strategies is based on the possibilities of analyzing data published on the state portal of the unified information system for public procurement. Open-access customer data is used to predict the coefficient of contract price reduction. An analysis of the scalar time series of this coefficient was carried out and the advantages of forecasting using an artificial neural network, namely, the NARX deep learning network, were shown. The initial maximum contract price is used as an exogenous parameter for the NARX neural network. Receiving data from the FTP server of the official website of public procurement is implemented by using the developed parser.

Title: Machine Learning Frameworks for Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT): A Comprehensive Analysis
Publication Title: 2022 First International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Information and Communication Technologies (ICEEICT)
Abstract Note: Many industrial processes have been transformed by ict infrastructure. Artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms have been needed by companies of all sizes, whether minor or major, to handle the terabytes of data created by sensing, actuation, production control systems, and web services. These data are large (terabytes) and diverse (picture, audio, video, graphics), necessitating the use of specific models and approaches for analysis and administration. Intelligent technologies create enormous quantities of data in massive industrial networks, hence IIoT frameworks demand clever, resilient big data analysis tools. Because of their cognitive acquisition and processing capabilities, artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) techniques generate impressive outcomes in IIoT networks. The possibility of machine learning in IIoT applications is assessed in this research study, which also gives a comprehensive topology of IIoT and major enabling technologies. The theoretical underpinnings of numerous well-known ML algorithms, as well as several software and hardware frameworks for ML implementations, are then presented. The use of machine learning techniques in IIoT applications is briefly covered. Finally, this study identifies key problems as well as potential research directions.

Author: X. Yang; C. Zhu
Title: Industrial Expert Systems Review: A Comprehensive Analysis of Typical Applications
Abstract Note: As a branch of artificial intelligence (AI), expert systems are well-known for interpreting and deducing solutions to problems based on the rules contained within a knowledge base. Over the past decade, the application of expert systems in the industrial sector has matured significantly, permeating both upstream and downstream segments of the supply chain. Unlike traditional methods in industry, the approach using expert systems leverages the knowledge within domain experts, simulating the decision-making process of experts through rules and inference engines, rather than solely relying on the intuition of manual operators or engineers. In this study, the recent progress in the industrial application of expert systems were reviewed, including 1) the fundamental concepts of expert systems (e.g. the components of expert systems, expert systems based on different reasoning mechanisms, and the developmental history of expert systems) were briefly introduced; 2) the advantages and limitations of expert systems were discussed; 3) a series of detailed examinations for the expert system application in areas such as risk assessment, decision analysis, industrial classification, and fault diagnosis were elucidated in this study; 4) The challenges currently faced by expert systems and their future development prospects were discussed. This study not only reviewed the application of expert systems in the industrial field but also revealed how these systems drive automated decision support, reduce dependence on manual experience, and enhance operational efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Furthermore, the study highlighted future development directions. Overall, the research emphasized the importance of effectively integrating expert systems in industrial automation and intelligent manufacturing, providing valuable insights for practitioners and researchers.

Author: A. A. Ibrahim; M. G. Abdulkareem; I. Al-Jadir
Title: Medical Drug Data Cluster Analysis Using Mimetic Optimization Technique Combined with Chaotic Logistic Maps
Publication Title: 2023 Al-Sadiq International Conference on Communication and Information Technology (AICCIT)
Abstract Note: Medical drug classification is considered one of the industrial applications that Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning can help to do that task. The Drug interactions, namely drug-drug (DDI), drug-target, and drug-food interactions, might stimulate unexpected adverse drug Effects (ADEs). In this paper, we propose a machine learning technique that utilizes Memetic optimization to efficiently classify the drugs category in an unsupervised manner. This is beneficial for pharmacists and people working in the pharmaceutical industries to have an automatic means that enable them to categorize drugs according to their chemical structures. This work combines the Evolutionary Algorithm with the Chaotic Logistic Maps to produce a global and local memetic hybridization. As a result, the proposed method outperformed the traditional k-means clustering in terms of the accuracy measure with an accuracy double obtained using the conventional method.

Author: S. U. Rahaman
Title: Internet of Things for Sustainable Carbon Footprint Reduction and Energy Management in Supply Chain
Abstract Note: The growing demand to address environmental sustainability and climate change has emphasized the need for innovative solutions in supply chain and energy management. This study investigates the transformative role of the Internet of Things (IoT) in reducing carbon footprints and optimizing energy utilization within supply chains. A well-structured methodology was employed including regression modeling, cluster analysis, IoT simulation frameworks and optimization techniques. The data was collected from diverse energy and emission databases. After exploratory data analysis and feature engineering, energy efficiency and emission intensity were identified as critical metrics. Regression models achieved an R² score of 0.89 and clustering revealing distinct industry patterns for targeted interventions. The IoT simulation framework demonstrated real-time and decision-making insights using MQTT protocols. Across repeated runs, median energy and CO₂ reductions are reported with uncertainty and maintained operational performance, These results showcase the potential of IoT-driven frameworks in providing actionable insights for sustainable supply chain management. This study also highlights the critical role of IoT in achieving sustainability goals and overcoming challenges associated with energy-intensive operations. This study will contribute in advancing the global agenda of sustainable development with measurable environmental benefits and operational improvements. It will also provide a robust framework for using IoT in achieving sustainability goals in other energy intensive sectors.

Author: A. M. Nair; N. S. Nath; S. U. S; S. S. A; A. Louis; R. Kumar V
Title: Proposal of Closed Loop Supply Chain Model for Medical Oxygen Cylinders
Abstract Note: A closed-loop supply chain (CLSC) is a supply chain management system that integrates forward logistics (from production to consumer) and reverse logistics (from consumer back to the manufacturer or appropriate disposal facilities). The goal of a CLSC is to maximize the value extracted from a product by ensuring that it is reused, re manufactured, recycled, or properly disposed of after its initial use. Oxygen cylinders, critical for healthcare services, require rigorous control in their distribution, usage, and return processes to ensure uninterrupted supply and compliance with health standards. The project involves analyzing the existing supply chain of A company in Kochuveli using data obtained through discussions and identifying bottlenecks in the reverse logistics process, and developing optimised strategies for the efficient collection, inspection and redistribution of oxygen cylinders. The major problems faced by this supply chain are Logistics Complexity, Data Management and Technology, Supply Chain Disruptions, Sustainability Concerns and Economic Viability. The project plans to study the existing supply chain and do comprehensive analysis of current supply chain operations, identification of potential recovery strategies, and the design of an optimized CLSC model. The various tools that can be used to solve the problems are Vehicle routing, Facility layout, Optimised path layout, Transportation analysis and Linear programming,ant colony optimization algorithm and other mathematical theorems. The expected outcomes are supposed to be increased efficiency and cost savings, enhanced sustainability, data driven decision making,optimized route and fleet management.

Author: A. Al-Hajri; A. Magid Hamouda; G. M. Abdella
Title: Sustainability-Based Strategic Framework for Digital Transformation in the Oil and Gas Industry
Abstract Note: The oil and gas industry is transforming profoundly, driven by integrating digital technologies to enhance sustainability, operational efficiency, and competitiveness. However, the sector continues to tackle persistent challenges, including fluctuating oil prices, the demand for a stable fuel supply, and increasingly stringent environmental regulations. This paper introduces a sustainability-centered strategic framework for digital transformation within the sector, showcasing the deployment of cutting-edge technologies like data analytics, artificial intelligence (AI), cloud computing, and the Internet of Things (IoT). These advancements address key industry challenges, including operational inefficiencies, safety hazards, and the pressing need to reduce carbon emissions. The research examines the hurdles faced by hydrocarbon-dependent countries, focusing on Qatar’s oil and gas sector, in adopting digital transformation. Through a qualitative case study method involving semi-structured interviews with industry experts, the study identifies critical barriers to digital transformation, organizational resistance to change, a shortage of skilled workers, cybersecurity concerns, and the complexities of integrating new technologies with legacy systems. The findings underscore the pivotal role of digital technologies in improving efficiency, safety, and sustainability, and they highlight the importance of strong leadership and workforce development. The proposed sustainability-based framework provides actionable insights for overcoming these barriers, offering a holistic approach to fostering innovation, enhancing resilience, and aligning digital initiatives with sustainable and business objectives. These results offer valuable guidance for industry leaders and policymakers navigating the complexities of digital transformation in a competitive and sustainable conscious global market.

Abstract Note: This paper examines the application of digital twin technology in predictive maintenance in an overall IoT industrial setting, presenting the technology in the improvement of operational performance, reducing operational interruptions, and ensuring maintenance cost efficiency. Digital twins allow for the development of up-to-date virtual images of tangible pieces of equipment which fosters data gathering and the application of predictive technologies for maintenance whereby possible failures are sorted out before they occur. The paper seeks to analyze broader aspects of the digital twins, for instance, the inclusiveness of specific machine learning artificial components like convolutional and recurrent neural networks in the technology, which increase the enablement of the predicted fault detection and maintenance scheduling. Consequently, the paper confronts the issues associated with the incorporation of a digital twin such as privacy of data, embedding to the preexisting systems, and the requirements of human resources with a particular skill set. In general, the results of the study suggest that digital twins are beneficial technological machines to assist in predictive maintenance with an incentive towards increasing efficiency within an industry and prolonging the useful life of equipment.

Implementing real-time automation and big data analytics has become vital for the oil and gas industry. Real-time automation refers to using a control system, sensors, and an IT solution to monitor or control operations in real-time. It leads to faster decision-making and efficiency in drilling, production, and distribution processes. At the same time, big data analytics is about storing and analyzing massive amounts of data from different sources—these are sensor data production processes for the oil and gas industry market trends. Big data analytics can intelligently detect patterns & trends with the help of sophisticated algorithms along with machine learning, based on which necessary insights are drawn that lead to better operations and decision-making. Author: Y. Kim; J. Lee; E. -B. Lee; J. -H. Lee
Title: Application of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Text-Mining of Big-Data to Engineering-Procurement-Construction (EPC) Bid and Contract Documents
Publication Title: 2020 6th Conference on Data Science and Machine Learning Applications (CDMA)
Abstract Note: Influenced by the innovative technological advances made in recent years, a number of major economies are increasing their national investment in building decision support systems applicable to the industrial sector. This study was conducted with government support to analyze big data in engineering accumulated from the past, develop tools for forecasting and risk management, and use them to build systems that support decision-making. The target of system development for this study is the industrial engineering sector, also known as Engineering- Procurement-Construction (EPC), and is largely divided into five modules, as follows. First, engineering design cost prediction targeted accurately predicting Man-Hour (M/H) and provided useful references to contractors and project managers by developing models that predict design costs in the engineering field. Using the M/H prediction model developed by this research, M/H under similar environments or conditions can be predicted and solutions prepared on the project start stage to manage potential risks. Second, engineering design error analysis was conducted with the purpose of helping project implementation risk management by developing a system to analyze actual design error data that occurred in past projects in order to minimize and prevent design errors. Third, engineering design change analysis was conducted to analyze amounts that could be added by changing order and preparing countermeasures in advance due to design changes occurring during the project. The system was built to support decision-making as a result of utilizing accumulated data. Fourth, engineering Invitation to Bid (ITB) analysis established a support system to efficiently perform ITB analysis and carry out new processes that automatically detect and present provisions that could pose risks. Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques were applied to conduct the analysis, and Subject-Matter Experts (SMEs) verified the quality during the process of establishing the procedure. Fifth, equipment maintenance cycle forecasting was conducted to predict the demand for repairing items and ensure that equipment requiring maintenance could be prepared before the predicted malfunction. This paper first introduces the five modules of decision support systems in a wide range, then details the results of the fourth module, the Engineering ITB Analysis step by step.

Title: Extended CSF-CoCoSo Method: A Novel Approach for Optimizing Logistics in the Oil and Gas Supply Chain
Abstract Note: In this research, we introduce a significant advancement in decision-making methodologies by proposing the Combine Compromise Solution Approach. This innovative method addresses the complexities inherent in multi-criteria decision-making scenarios, particularly in optimizing logistics within the oil and gas supply chain. Through our exploration of circular spherical fuzzy sets, we investigate various algebraic operations without detailing the research methodology. Our primary contribution lies in the practical application and effectiveness of the Combine Compromise Solution Approach, exemplified through a case study on logistics optimization. By presenting insights gleaned from this approach and conducting a comparative analysis against alternative methods, we demonstrate its utility and potential impact in real-world contexts. This research not only offers immediate practical solutions but also paves the way for future investigations into the broader applications of circular spherical fuzzy sets in decision-making processes, thus advancing the field significantly.

Author: V. Hassija; V. Chamola; V. Gupta; S. Jain; N. Guizani
Title: A Survey on Supply Chain Security: Application Areas, Security Threats, and Solution Architectures
Abstract Note: The rapid improvement in the global connectivity standards has escalated the level of trade taking place among different parties. Advanced communication standards are allowing the trade of all types of commodities and services. Furthermore, the goods and services developed in a particular region are transcending boundaries to enter into foreign markets. Supply chains play an essential role in the trade of these goods. To be able to realize a connected world with no boundary restrictions in terms of goods and services, it is imperative to keep the associated supply chains transparent, secure, and trustworthy. Therefore, some fundamental changes in the current supply chain architecture are essential to achieve a secure trade environment. This article discusses the supply chain's security-critical application areas and presents a detailed survey of the security issues in the existing supply chain architecture. Various emerging technologies, such as blockchain, machine learning (ML), and physically unclonable functions (PUFs) as solutions to the vulnerabilities in the existing infrastructure of the supply chain have also been discussed. Recent studies reviewed in this work reveal a growing sentiment in the industry toward new and emerging technologies, such as Internet of Things (IoT), blockchain, and ML. While many organizations have already adopted IoT applications and artificial intelligence systems in their businesses, widespread adoption of blockchain remains distant. It has also been found that over the past decade, PUF-based authentication systems have gained much ground. However, a proper reference model for their implementation in complex supply chains is still missing.

Title: Securing the Petroleum Supply Chain Through Proof of Work Implementation

Abstract Note: The petroleum supply chain in India involves various players from crude oil exploration to refined product distribution, creating a complex and competitive environment. This study demonstrates the implementation of a Proof of Work (PoW) mechanism in the petroleum supply chain using python, defining Block and Blockchain classes, creating a Proof of Work mechanism, and simulating transactions. The experimental results, using different difficulty levels, show that increasing the number of leading zeros in the hash significantly raises computational complexity and time, thereby strengthening the blockchain's tamper-resistance. This approach ensures a reliable and transparent record of petroleum transactions from extraction to end-user delivery.

Title: Optimizing Supply Chain Operations through Machine Learning: Opportunities and Challenges
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Pervasive Computational Technologies (ICPCT)
Abstract Note: In order to create enduring partnerships that are essential to attaining long-term economic success, supply chain management, or SCM, is essential. Strict standards and decision-making procedures are necessary for efficient SCM, and they have a big influence on the final results. In order to efficiently manage inventory levels and logistical procedures, this study offers a complex ML system that combines demand forecasts and optimisation methods. For predicting demand, the approach uses a Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) model, taking advantage of its capacity to process time-series data and identify intricate trends. The algorithm achieves an impressive 96.4% forecasting of demand accuracy by examining extensive historical sales data and a variety of supply chain variables. In order to optimise inventory rules, the investigation also uses optimisation techniques, particularly Genetic Algorithms (GA) in conjunction with Simulated Annealing (SA). The model demonstrated significant improvements when implemented to an actual commercial logistics system. Notably, it reduced storage expenses by 19% and increased order fulfillment rates by 24%, proving its capacity for combining service standards with financial efficiency. The findings highlight the significant impact of ML on improving supply chain efficiency and show how it can result in significant cost savings and improve service quality.

Author: Sindi, Abdulaziz; Paik, Jeom-Kee
Title: Digital Healthcare Engineering System for Enhancing the Safety and Sustainability of Aging Monopile Offshore Wind Turbines in Storm Conditions
Abstract Note: Offshore wind turbines are operating in harsh metocean environments, where aging assets face compounding deterioration across the structure. In-service damage mechanisms (corrosion, fatigue cracking, and denting) are exacerbated by these metocean conditions, elevating risks to safety, reliability, and operational performance. Conventional maintenance strategies often prove inadequate, particularly during extreme storm conditions. This study presents a Digital Healthcare Engineering (DHE) framework for aging monopile offshore wind turbines. The framework is a lifecycle-oriented, real-time management system driven by artificial intelligence (AI) and Digitalization, designed to enhance the safety and sustainability of aging engineered structures (including ships, offshore installations, and offshore wind turbines) while safeguarding the occupational health and well-being of seafarers and offshore workers. By shifting asset management from reactive to proactive, the DHE system enables early failure detection, optimizes maintenance planning, and reduces the risk of catastrophic failure. The framework is scalable beyond monopile-supported turbines to other aging offshore and marine structures, supporting safer, more resilient, and sustainable operations.Offshore wind turbines have become essential in the global transition to clean energy, with projected global capacity rising from 75 GW to 2,000 GW by 2050 (World Economic Forum, 2025). Monopile foundations, which currently support approximately 63% to 81% of these installations, remain the predominant foundation type for fixed-bottom OWTs due to their simplicity, cost-efficiency, and established installation procedures (Fabricius, 2023; Vidal et al., 2020). However, as turbine sizes increase and wind farms are developed in deeper waters, these structures face heightened design demands and operational challenges (Fabricius, 2023; Horwath et al., 2020). Offshore wind turbines are continually exposed to harsh marine conditions, including wind, waves, and currents, which induce significant cyclic and extreme loading over their lifetimes. These loads are compounded by environmental degradation mechanisms such as general and localized corrosion, fatigue cracking, and accidental mechanical damage (TNO, 2023; Xie et al., 2025; Sindi et al., 2024b). Fig. 1 illustrates these challenges in real-world offshore conditions and their impact on OWT structural integrity. Corrosion, especially in splash zones and internal compartments exposed by failed seals, reduces material thickness and load-carrying capacity (Sunday and Brennan, 2021; Sindi et al., 2024a) Fatigue, triggered by wind and wave-induced cyclic loading, leads to crack initiation and propagation, particularly at welds or other stress concentrators (TNO, 2023; Oyeniran and Aziaka, 2020). Moreover, mechanical damage from vessel collisions or dropped tools can introduce local denting, reducing structural integrity and potentially initiating further fatigue failure (Xie et al., 2025; Cho et al., 2013). These degradation mechanisms often interact synergistically, as seen in corrosion-fatigue, where pitting corrosion accelerates fatigue crack growth and significantly shortens fatigue life (TNO, 2023; Okenyi et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). At the same time, environmental factors such as scour erosion reduce effective pile embedment, changing soil-structure interaction and diminishing the foundation’s lateral stiffness and capacity under extreme storm loading (Wei et al., 2024). Inspection and maintenance are further complicated by the offshore environment. Traditional condition assessment approaches, typically based on periodic inspections or predefined maintenance schedules, are logistically intensive, hazardous, and often insufficient for managing aging structures that exceed their initial 20–25 year design life (Sindi et al., 2024b). In this context, conventional SHM techniques may fail to offer the real-time insight or long-term degradation forecasting necessary for life extension and safe operation.

Author: Yi, Cong; Lv, Baicheng; Gao, Wei; Mao, Jingxuan
Title: Operational State Analysis of Semi-Submersible Floating Wind Turbines Based on Measured Data
Abstract Note: Semi-submersible floating wind turbines serving in deep and distant waters face complex marine environments. So monitoring and analyzing their environmental and motion responses are of great significance to ensure the safe operation and maintenance of the platform and the subsequent optimization of its design. Based on the marine environment and motion data measured by integrated marine monitoring system of a wind turbine platform, the environmental conditions and platform inclination distribution characteristics of the wind turbine platform are analyzed, and the motion response of the platform under typhoon conditions is studied. Finally, a hydrodynamic simulation model of the platform was established, and the predicted results of the model were compared with the measured results. The results of the study provide a reference for the safe operation and maintenance of this platform and the optimized design of similar platforms.Renewable energy technologies, particularly wind and solar, are central to the global energy transition. Nowadays, Renewable energy resources are now rapidly replacing fossil fuels and becoming a major source of eco-friendly electricity production(Sukanta, 2022). They are expected to supply two-thirds of the global energy demand by 2050, playing a crucial role in reducing greenhouse gas emissions to limit global temperature rise(Gielen, 2019; Resch, 2008).Due to the special characteristics of floating wind turbine platforms, they can collect wind energy resources in the deep and distant sea(Barooni, 2022), but they also put higher requirements on the safety of the platforms(He, 2024). Field monitoring of offshore structures plays a vital role in reducing the risk of structural failure , optimizing the design of platforms and guaranteeing the success of offshore operations(Peng, 2017).At present, more scholars have carried out in-depth studies on the field monitoring of floating platforms and analyzed the data obtained. The clustering algorithm can be performed from the current monitoring data to obtain extreme velocity pole profiles(Lyu, 2023). Using the monitored platform transverse and longitudinal oscillation data, deep learning algorithms can be utilized to extract data features and thus accomplish the prediction of platform motion(Li, 2023). Integrating motion monitoring data with simulated motion predictions of floating offshore wind turbines results in less uncertainty and higher accuracy in short-term extreme platform motions(Li, 2024). Field monitoring data can be compared with the digital twin to validate accuracy and to estimate fatigue loads on the platform to inform operations and decision- making(Branlard, 2024).
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Liu, Yihua; Wang, Junyi
Title: Studies on Early Warning Models and Ship Navigation State Monitoring Using Multi-Source Data
Abstract Note: This study develops a vessel navigation monitoring and early warning system by integrating multi-source data, including environmental and vessel-specific sensors. Initial processing uses wavelet threshold denoising on environmental data to enhance quality. Subsequently, data is combined with the Automatic Identification System (AIS) information, and the integrated dataset is analyzed using an entropy-weighted cloud model for precise risk assessment. This approach significantly improves the accuracy of maritime navigation safety and risk prediction.Maritime transportation is an important pillar of global trade (Guo et al. 2022), and ensuring the safety, efficiency, and environmental friendliness of ships navigating in complex marine environments has always been a core concern of the shipping industry. With the increasing frequency of global shipping activities, the risks faced by ships during navigation are also increasing (He et al. 2024). For example, adverse meteorological conditions, changes in the hydrographic environment, and increases in the density of maritime traffic may significantly affect the navigational status of ships. How to reduce the risk of maritime traffic through effective monitoring and warning mechanisms has become a common research focus in both academia and industry.Currently, the data sources for the monitoring and control technology of ship navigation status mainly include automatic identification system (AIS) data, meteorological sensor information, and dynamic and static parameters of ships. However, it is difficult for a single data source to comprehensively reflect the complex navigational environment in which the ship is located (Piotr, 2017), and there may be noise or inconsistency in the data, which affects the accuracy and real-time performance of the monitoring and warning system. In this regard, the construction of a ship navigation state monitoring and warning model based on the fusion of multi-source information can provide more comprehensive and accurate risk assessment and warning support based on the integration of multi-dimensional data. In recent years, big data analysis techniques and machine learning algorithms have been widely used. Based on this, this study aims to integrate multi-source information such as meteorological conditions, ship dynamic and static parameters, and to construct a real-time monitoring and risk warning model by adopting advanced data processing techniques, such as wavelet threshold denoising, and classification algorithms, such as K-nearest neighbor classifier (Wang, 2022). The model is not only able to accurately monitor the state of the ship in the complex navigational environment, but also can realize rapid assessment and early warning of the risk through risk feature extraction and fusion, so as to provide scientific and reliable decision-making support for ship pilots.
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Cao, Ke; Wang, Jinguang; Ye, Maokun; Wan, Decheng
Title: Prediction of Mooring Line Tension of a FOWT Under Uniform Wind and Irregular Waves by Artificial Neural Networks
Abstract Note: As a crucial component in Floating Offshore Wind Turbines (FOWTs), mooring systems are used to constrain the displacement of the structures. In numerical simulations of FOWTs subjected to combined wave-wind loads, accurate prediction of the mooring-line forces is of great importance to the prediction of platform motions. This paper presents a numerical framework for predicting the tension forces of mooring lines of an FOWT by leveraging artificial neural networks. The well-validated in-house code naoe-FOAM-SJTU developed for marine applications at Shanghai Jiao Tong University based on the open-source software OpenFOAM, is employed to perform high-fidelity numerical simulations to generate the training dataset for neural networks. A potential-viscous flow combined strategy is adopted to simulate the platform motions and mooring-line tensions of a real-scale FOWT under uniform wind and irregular waves. A feature selection process is conducted to screen those variables which influence the mooring-line tensions most. Afterward, those screened variables are fed into the neural networks as inputs to infer the mooring-line tensions. It is shown that the neural network model trained by the proposed methodology has good performance for some lines, demonstrating the potential of the proposed framework in the prediction of mooring line tensions of FOWTs.For some reasons about geographical location and resource development, it tends to the deep sea for the development of floating offshore wind turbine (FOWT). For FOWT, the mooring system takes an indispensable part in positioning to ensure the safe operation of the entire system. Under the real sea state, the FOWT is usually exposed to some complicated and severe environmental conditions such as winds, waves, currents, combined wind and wave conditions and so on, which can induce floating platform six-degree-of-freedom (6DOF) motions and loads on the mooring lines (Jeon et al., 2023). Furthermore, the tension of the mooring lines change, which constrain the motions. Actually, the mooring line failures occur frequently, which influence the navigational safety and operation of the FOWTs. Thus, especially for semi-submersible FOWTs, more and more researches about the impact of the mooring line failure have been conducted (Tang et al., 2025). Besides, optimizing the mooring system design properly is an effective method to improve overall stability and safety (Chen et al., 2022).
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Kyrtatos, Panagiotis; Nikolopoulos, Lampros; Nikolopoulos, Pavlos; Simatos, Panagiotis; Chatzidakis, Konstantinos
Title: DiEM – A Digital Engine Management Platform for Condition-Based Maintenance and Performance Optimization
Abstract Note: The LNG shipping industry faces mounting pressures from decarbonization regulations, supply chain disruptions, and increasing engine complexity. To address these challenges, Propulsion Analytics and GasLog co-developed an Engine Condition-Based Maintenance (ECBM) scheme which is hosted on the Digital Engine Management (DiEM) platform. DiEM integrates high-frequency engine data, physical inspections, lubricant and water analyses with physics-based digital twin modeling and machine learning to assess engine health, predict faults, and optimize maintenance. This paper outlines the ECBM framework, detailing its data acquisition, diagnostic methodologies, and integration with maintenance systems.The evolution of shipping companies and vessel types reflects their ongoing efforts to adapt to shifting global trade demands, new financing models, technological advancements, and growing environmental pressures. To remain agile and competitive, many shipping firms operate fleets that span a wide range of ship types and sizes. While this diversity enhances operational flexibility, it also introduces increased complexity in fleet management and planning.Traditionally, shipboard crews were responsible for engine maintenance. However, the sophistication of today's marine engines – including advanced automation, electronic control systems, and high-performance components – has surpassed the capabilities of standard crew training. These systems now require specialized technical expertise.Machinery maintenance has also undergone significant transformation, moving from reactive repairs after failure to preventive maintenance based on operating time. This time-based maintenance relies on manufacturer-prescribed service intervals, grounded in theoretical lifetimes beyond which failure probability escalates (ISO 2016). Yet, these assumptions often carry large uncertainties – particularly for the niche machinery used in ocean-going vessels. As a result, maintenance may be performed too early, leading to avoidable costs, or too late, increasing the risk of failure. Moreover, scheduled overhauls of non-inspectable components can inadvertently introduce faults during disassembly or reassembly.To address the limitations of time-based maintenance, condition-based approaches – collectively known as Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM) – have emerged (Teixeira, Lopes, and Braga 2020; Klockars, Eykerman, and Mayr 2011). CBM offers shipowners significant advantages, including reduced costs, less equipment downtime, better engine performance, and more predictable operations. Remote diagnostics and predictive maintenance systems can substantially cut maintenance expenses by extending service intervals, while also enhancing safety, reliability, and lowering greenhouse gas emissions. Further savings can arise from improved engine efficiency and reduced fuel consumption (Kyrtatos 2024; Basurko and Uriondo 2015). Despite these advantages, CBM can entail higher upfront or long-term investment, making cost optimization critical. Ship operators must efficiently manage procurement, inspections, overhauls, and spare parts while leveraging in-house expertise and advanced software tools. These systems must also support the complexity of mixed fleets with varied ship and engine configurations.
Conference Name: SNAME Maritime Convention

Author: Zhang, Yihan; Hu, Xiaobing; Wang, Ping
Title: The Extreme Short-Term Prediction of Motions for Two Parallel Ships in Waves by Deep-Learning
Abstract Note: The coupled motion of two ships in waves are calculated by 3D potential flow theory in time domain, and one-hour time history are obtained for deep-learning. Then, three kinds of deep-learning models are established for extreme short-term prediction for motion of two ships simultaneously, respectively artificial neural network (ANN), long short-term memory network (LSTM) and temporal convolutional network (TCN). Through the optimization and comparison, TCN model has the best accuracy, and the mean respective error is less than 20% for 10 seconds prediction in advance.With the development of ship and offshore engineering, the operation at sea become more complex. The collaborative operation of multiple floating bodies become frequent, such as ship replenishment, heavy equipment lifting of offshore wind turbine and emergency personnel transfer at sea. The hydrodynamic interference has obvious influence to ship motion for two parallel ships, and the relative movement of two ships can threaten the security of the collaborative work. The accurate prediction or online forecasting can assist the operation.There are a lot of researches on the coupled motions of two ships, and the potential theory is the main method because of its high efficiency. Ohkusu (1976) firstly studied the hydrodynamic interaction between two floating bodies with series expansion and 2D potential method, which is generalized to two slender bodies in oblique sea by Kodan (1984). Chen et al. (1997) studied the hydrodynamic problem of advanced two parallel with 3D pulsation source. Then, the moving pulsation source was used by McTaggart et al. (2003), and he found that the hydrodynamic interaction for small ship is always more violent than that for big ship by comparison of model test. Rankine source was used to solve this problem by Yuan et al. (2014) and Zhou et al. (2014). For wave force resonance and peaking effect problem on small gap of two floating bodies, the 3D potential theory with pulsation source was used by Miao et al. (2000). With the development of CFD method, the interaction effect can be discussed in viscous flow, and the RANS equation was used for wave-making force and interaction force calculation in still water, which can be used for the analysis of the motion and force characteristics of different ship to ship positions.
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Clark, A.B.; Pyrak-Nolte, L.J.; Nolte, D.D.
Title: Characterizing Fracture Asperity Distribution in the Laboratory with Internal Seismic Sources and Triplet Neural Networks
Abstract Note: Reliable energy production and anthropogenic waste isolation depend on the ability to accurately gauge fluid flow through fractures in rock. Direct measurements of fracture properties within deep subsurface reservoirs are not possible. However, remote sensing of natural and induced seismicity represents a promising approach because seismic waves contain information on the complex media through which they propagate. Here, we study the ability to extract information on the asperity distribution in a single fracture using passive monitoring of waves from sources internal to the fracture. A synthetic parallel-plate fracture was created from acrylic blocks separated by acrylic rods that represented asperities. Chattering dust was used to generate numerous acoustic emissions from within the fracture. A triplet neural network (TNN) was applied to the recorded signals for dimensionality reduction. The TNN identified features of the spatial frequency of the periodic asperity distributions that occurred at the arrival time of a Scholte wave. The work presented here demonstrates the potential for identifying changes in fracture geometry by evaluating the scattered fields from signals generated internally in a fracture.
Conference Name: 59th U.S. Rock Mechanics/Geomechanics Symposium

Author: Muqtadir, A.; Zhang, B.; Ben Saleh, W.; Chalaturnyk, R.; Vaisblat, N.; Wigston, A.; Drobot, A.; Groenewoud, L.; Kirsten, M.
Title: Geothermal reservoir modeling and calibration in a coupled thermo-hydro-mechanical perspective - a case study for DEEP Geothermal Project in Saskatchewan, Canada
Abstract Note: Geothermal offers a low-emission, reliable and sustainable source of base-load energy, yet efficient reservoir management is challenging. Understanding the Thermo (T), Hydro (H) and Mechanical (M) interactions can help create sustainable strategies and ease energy extraction. In this study, a geological regional-scale THM model is created using PETREL, based on geological data from 11 wells close to the DEEP geothermal project. Geostatistical modelling was challenging as only 11 wells are available for such a large area. The depositional directions are used as secondary data for stratigraphic modeling. The permeability was populated using Machine Learning techniques based on 9 well logs as input data and calibrated with pulse-decay tests. A Mechanical Earth Model (MEM) was set up using a combination of empirical equations and calibrated using laboratory and field data. The minimum horizontal stress in the sandstone reservoir was found to be lower (~41 MPa) compared with caprock shale. Two open geothermal loop tests were history matched using INTERSECT which confirm fractures are induced for improved transmissibility in the sandstone reservoir without causing caprock failures.
Conference Name: 59th U.S. Rock Mechanics/Geomechanics Symposium

Author: Zhu, Jiacen; Wang, FuMing; Yang, Fan; Fu, Zhengze; Li, Mingshun
Title: Phase-Based Offshore Wind Power Prediction Using LSTM Neural Networks
Abstract Note: Offshore wind power plays a crucial role in renewable energy development. This study proposes a phase-based Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network power prediction model, utilizing high-frequency SCADA system data. Turbine operations are divided into five phases: Shutdown, Standby, Start-stop Transition, Medium Speed, and Rated Speed. To ensure robust model evaluation, a baseline model is established as a reference. Validation results demonstrate that the proposed LSTM model outperforms traditional deep learning methods, significantly improving the accuracy of offshore wind power prediction.With the rapid development of the global economy, energy demand is continuously increasing. As traditional energy sources, such as oil and coal, approach depletion and the greenhouse effect becomes increasingly significant, the development and utilization of renewable energy have become the optimal solution to global energy challenges (Roga et al, 2022). Wind energy as a clean, environmentally friendly, and renewable resource, can be broadly categorized as a form of solar energy. Owing to its cleanliness, substantial environmental benefits, and short infrastructure development cycle, wind energy is gradually replacing coal-fired power as the preferred choice for modern power systems (Yang et al, 2023).Compared to the nearly satur1ated onshore wind energy, offshore wind power represents a critical direction in the global energy transition. Stable offshore resources and the robust economies of coastal regions provide a favorable environment for the development of offshore wind power. Over the past two decades, the offshore wind energy industry has gradually transitioned from the demonstration phase to the commercialization phase, garnering increasing attention worldwide (Chen et al, 2022). However, the development of offshore wind power is significantly constrained by the uncertain and complex marine environment. During its service life, offshore wind power output exhibits considerable variability and is challenging to predict (Li et al, 2020). Therefore, short-term wind power forecasting for offshore wind farms has attracted widespread attention from researchers, leading to a substantial body of literature on wind power prediction. High precision wind power forecasting not only enhances the stability of wind power integration into the grid but also effectively mitigates the over-discharge issues in wind energy storage systems.
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Zhou, Shiyang; Lyu, Pin; Wang, Hu; Xu, Shengwen
Title: On the Wind Turbine Clearance Forecasting Surrogate Model
Abstract Note: This study develops a computationally efficient surrogate model for predicting blade tower clearance in a custom wind turbine model, with a focus on blade deformation under varying wind conditions. Utilizing open source software OpenFAST from NREL for simulation and MATLAB's Deep Learning Toolbox for neural network training, the model is based on over 37 million data points. A Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) model, tailored for high and low wind-speed regimes, demonstrates robust performance with a prediction error of approximately 5%. This approach enhances prediction accuracy and offers a cost-effective solution for turbine performance forecasting in engineering applications.Wind energy is pivotal in transitioning to sustainable energy systems, requiring accurate prediction of blade deformation and clearance to ensure operational safety and efficiency. Traditional methods like Blade Element Momentum (BEM) theory and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) face challenges in computational efficiency and real-time accuracy. Recent advancements in artificial intelligence offer promising solutions. Neural networks, particularly physics-informed models (Li & Zhang, 2022) and multilayer perceptrons (Lalonde et al., 2022), have demonstrated high precision in predicting aerodynamic loads and structural responses with reduced computational costs. Studies highlight their superiority in blade deformation prediction (Salehi & Burgueño, 2018; Stetco et al., 2020), while adaptive neuro-fuzzy systems and MLPs excel in wind power forecasting (Colak et al., 2012). By leveraging extensive simulation data, these models bypass traditional limitations, providing robust alternatives for real-time applications.This study focuses on the self-developed wind turbine model, aiming to develop a high-precision, high-efficiency surrogate model for predicting blade deformation and clearance, thereby mitigating the risk of tower strikes. The research leverages aerodynamic load and structural deformation data generated by the open-source software OpenFAST from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) and trains and optimizes two Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) network models. By segmenting predictions into low and high wind speed regions, a hybrid approach is employed to predict blade tip deformation, significantly enhancing prediction accuracy across the entire wind speed spectrum. This paper presents the construction of the hybrid neural network and evaluates its predictive performance, achieving an average normalized root mean square error (NRMSE) of 5.213%. Compared to a single neural network model, this hybrid approach improves prediction accuracy by 5.208%, with a notable improvement of 16.306% in the low wind speed range.
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Žuškin, Srdan; Vilic, Ivan; Šakan, Davor; Valcic, Sanjin
Title: Generating and Testing Navigation Scenarios for Verifying COLREG-Compliant Decision Support System
Abstract Note: The International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREG) compliant Decision Support Systems (DSS) with an artificially intelligent collision avoidance system is crucial to achieving automated ships in the near future. Furthermore, DSS systems will be integrated into the current primary means of navigation, the Integrated Navigation System (INS), to enhance navigation safety and environmental protection. The system will utilize data collected from onboard navigation sensors and incorporate sensor data from multiple sources in DSS through advanced digital technology.Generating and testing navigation scenarios to verify the COLREG-compliant DSS necessitates rigorous adherence to the COLREG rules. Additionally, this study offers an extensive framework for generating and testing various navigation scenarios to validate COLREG-compliant DSS systems in open sea conditions.Each generated scenario is created with the marine stakeholders using the Full Mission Bridge (FMB) navigation simulator. The navigational scenarios simulate realistic conditions, ensuring the DSS can make appropriate and timely decisions to avoid collisions and navigate safely. Since the COLREG consists of different obligations tailored to various navigational situations, following different ship encounters defined, reflecting the different facets of the regulations will be essential as a crucial step towards enhancing maritime safety by developing advanced, COLREG-compliant navigation support technologies, facing an autonomous future.The autonomous future starts with the concept of Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS), as defined by the International Maritime Organization (IMO), which refers to vessels that can operate with varying degrees of automation, ranging from decision-support systems for human operators to fully autonomous ships with no onboard crew. The IMO in MSC.1/Circ.1638 (2021) has categorized MASS operations into four levels: (1) ships with automated processes and decision support, where seafarers are still on board; (2) remotely controlled ships with seafarers on board; (3) remotely controlled ships without seafarers on board; and (4) fully autonomous ships that can make decisions and operate independently. As the maritime industry moves toward higher levels of automation, developing DSS solutions that comply with COLREG rules becomes a critical step in ensuring safe navigation in mixed environments where conventional and autonomous vessels coexist.
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Hosamo, Haidar; Hosamo, Mohsen; Dai, Jian; Jiang, Zhiyu
Title: Bridging Data Gaps with Machine Learning for Reliable Monitoring of a Floating Solar Array Under Extreme Marine Environments
Abstract Note: An experimental campaign was conducted at Universidad Politécnica de Madrid using a scaled floating photovoltaic (FPV) array subjected to various wave conditions to examine the technical feasibility of the concept. Optical sensors were instrumented to track the motions of PV panels at different locations in an array of 216 interconnected panels. A challenge of sensor data discontinuity in the FPV array is observed due to wave-induced motions under harsh marine environments. To deal with these challenges, machine learning (ML) models, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Artificial Neural Networks, Extreme Gradient Boosting, and Decision Trees, were implemented to predict and reconstruct missing sensor data collected during these tests. Among these, LSTM networks showed the highest predictive accuracy, achieving a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 0.001. These results highlight the potential of advanced ML methods in enhancing the resilience, reliability, and operational efficiency of renewable energy infrastructures in dynamic marine environments.The dynamic nature of renewable energy landscapes is intensifying the need for advanced solutions to manage the integration and reliability of such systems. According to the International Energy Outlook 2023 (IEO2023) by the U.S. Energy Information Administration, global energy demand is projected to rise by 34% from 638 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2022 to 855 quads by 2050 under the Reference case (International Energy Outlook, 2024). Det Norske Veritas forecasts that by 2050, variable renewable energy sources will comprise 70% of the global power system, with solar capacity increasing 22-fold, propelled by its cost efficiency and pivotal role in decarbonization and energy security (The Rise of Renewables, 2024). In the sphere of photovoltaic (PV) systems, innovations such as floating PV (FPV) systems are gaining traction (Ghosh, 2023). These systems utilize large water bodies to host photovoltaic setups, particularly beneficial in areas with limited land but abundant water resources (Dai et al., 2020; López et al., 2022). However, deploying these systems in coastal (Zhang et al., 2024) and offshore (Jiang et al., 2023) areas introduces unique challenges due to variable wave conditions, which can impact the stability and efficiency of FPVs and disrupt sensor data crucial for monitoring these installations (Arora et al., 2023).
Conference Name: The 35th International Ocean and Polar Engineering Conference

Author: Ducamp, Gaspard; Kim, Peter; Ruth, Eivind
Title: Federated Learning for the Maritime and Offshore Industries: Training Machine Learning Models Without Data Sharing
Abstract Note: In the fast-evolving field of machine learning, data privacy and security are critical concerns, especially in sensitive and heavily regulated sectors such as offshore energy and maritime operations. Traditional centralized machine learning approaches require consolidating all data in a single location for model training, which can introduce substantial privacy risks and often conflict with regulatory requirements. Federated Learning (FL) allows data to remain decentralized while enabling collaborative and individual model development in industry. FL is particularly valuable for two key use cases. First, multiple companies with a shared interest in developing a generalized model can collaborate through FL while reducing data privacy concerns. Second, a single company with distributed data—whether due to regulatory restrictions or logistical challenges—can leverage FL to train a unified model across its datasets without centralizing data on one server. By keeping data localized, FL enhances privacy, regulatory compliance, and operational flexibility, making it well-suited for the needs of the maritime and offshore industries. Popularized by Google, FL not only lowers privacy concerns but also strengthens model robustness and accuracy by drawing on diverse, distributed data sources. This capability is particularly valuable in offshore and maritime applications, where varied data from different installations, vessels, and sea conditions can inform more reliable predictive insights.In this paper, we discuss the challenges and opportunities of applying Federated Learning to the offshore and maritime industries. We present an overview of different applications, including examples from recent studies and toy cases that demonstrate FL's potential for these industries. We illustrate the value of FL further through the case of a container ship navigating various seas, where we aim to predict the vessel's performance under different conditions. Additionally, we describe a working procedure using an open-source package to implement FL, tested in a real-world setting with participants retaining data locally. A server hosted by a cloud provider was used to coordinate the Federated Learning process. This example demonstrates the strength of Federated Learning in developing generalized and robust models—a crucial need in the offshore and maritime sectors, where diverse and adaptable models are essential to address complex and variable real-world conditions. By maintaining data locally for each participant, FL emerges as a promising approach for developing regulatory-compliant, data-driven solutions for critical maritime applications.
Conference Name: SNAME 30th Offshore Symposium

Author: Xu, Ersi; Yu, Ting; Chen, Liqing; Ren, Xiaoxu; Wang, Shouyi; Wang, Gaige; Wu, Kehan; Pang, Chunxiao; Tong, Yanming; Liu, Peng; Pan, Yuanwei
Title: Integrated Live Geomechanics Model to Unlock the Deep Shale Gas Reservoir
Abstract Note: Shale gas reservoirs have become a significant energy source due to their commercial natural gas production. However, their inherent complexity poses numerous challenges for effective development. In this study, we introduce an integrated real-time geomechanics modeling workflow for deep shale gas reservoirs. This workflow utilizes extensive static and dynamic data, including various seismic attributes, precise 3D pore pressure models, anisotropic characterization and real-time micro-seismic data. This newly proposed workflow optimizes the quality of the geomechanics model. By adjusting the geosteering model for horizontal wells and correlating it with the true stratigraphic thickness (TST) domain, we achieve more accurate 3D structural modeling. Utilizing machine learning techniques, we extracted seismic attributes correlated with logging data to construct a reliable fracture model, which guides geomechanical modeling and hydraulic fracturing model. This model informs the design of geomechanics characterization and hydraulic fracturing. By iteratively updating the geological and geomechanical models with real-time micro-seismic data and engineering analysis, we achieved more precise stress characteristics and reservoir properties. The study results indicate that this integrated real-time geomechanics model, compatible with horizontal well drilling data, significantly improves stress accuracy, identifies sweet spots in the reservoir, and provides robust support for reservoir development.There are abundant marine shale gas resources in the Sichuan basin, located in southwestern China, form a large and complex superimposed basin on the northwestern edge of the Yangtze Plate. There are six main sets of hydrocarbon source strata in the Sichuan Basin. Among them, the Ordovician Wufeng Formation—Silurian Longmaxi Formation marine shale are the most favorable strata for exploration and development with stable distribution and excellent quality (He et al., 2023). The burial depth of deep shale gas reservoirs in the southern Sichuan Basin ranges from 3500 to 4500 meters. These reservoirs are characterized by very complex geological conditions, including developed faults and fractures and high in-situ horizontal stresses.
Conference Name: International Geomechanics Conference

Author: Musa, I. H.; Tan, C. P.; Leem, J.
